topic.title: Thoughts on standardizing build backend support for non-standard targets

post.authored_text:
I'm pretty certain most, if not all, of those only do so because they date back to when setuptools tried to be the whole workflow instead of just a wheel builder and `python setup.py test` and the like were the norm.

Designing these tools to latch onto setuptools allowed them to *borrow* configuration like project name and version but the move to putting core metadata in the backend agnostic pyproject.toml's `[project]` section ([pep 621](https://peps.python.org/pep-0621/)) superseded that. If you look at [cxfreeze's usage guide](https://cx-freeze.readthedocs.io/en/stable/setup_script.html) you can see that, whilst the old `setup.py` based way is still there, the default has completely moved on; it's fully decoupled itself from setuptools using the shared `[project]` configuration for common metadata, its own configuration for the rest and its own standalone `cxfreeze build` command.

projected_parent_summary.summary: I’m exploring whether non‑standard build targets should be standardized, noting that many developers still use older systems and that adoption by frontends like uv, hatch, and pdm is uncertain. I’d appreciate feedback from frontend developers on whether this standardization would be useful.
projected_parent_summary.important_uncertainty: I can't definitively say this one way of the other

phrase_candidates.text: I can't definitively say this one way of the other
I would love to get some feedback from any of these frontend developers if this is something they can see being standardized.
